audit_status.review_note: Reviewed verse by verse for major Hebrew wording, grammar, context, and interpretive translation choices. Final scholarly review may still refine individual renderings and textual-variant notes.

verses.restored: So the heavens and the earth were completed, and all their host.

familiar.text: Thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them.

notes.title: Host
notes.body: Host means the ordered fullness or array of creation: heavens, earth, living beings, lights, and all that belongs to them.

verses.restored: On the seventh day, the Divine completed the work that had been made, and rested on the seventh day from all the work that had been made.

familiar.text: And on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made; and he rested on the seventh day from all his work which he had made.

notes.title: Completed and rested
notes.body: The Hebrew wording links completion with rest. Rest is not absence or laziness; it is the sacred completion of creative work.

notes.title: Textual note
notes.body: Some ancient witnesses read that the Divine completed the work on the sixth day, but the Masoretic reading says seventh. The received Hebrew text is preserved here.

verses.restored: And the Divine blessed the seventh day and made it sacred, because on it the Divine rested from all the work of creating that had been made.

familiar.text: And God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it: because that in it he had rested from all his work which God created and made.

notes.title: First sacred thing
notes.body: The first thing explicitly made sacred is not a place, priesthood, building, or doctrine. It is time, marked by rest.

verses.restored: These are the generations of the heavens and the earth when they were created, in the day that the Eternal Divine made earth and heavens.

familiar.text: These are the generations of the heavens and of the earth when they were created, in the day that the LORD God made the earth and the heavens,

notes.title: Generations
notes.body: Toledot introduces an account, lineage, or unfolding record. Here it bridges the cosmic creation account into the garden account.

notes.title: The Eternal Divine
notes.body: This is the first appearance in Genesis of YHWH Elohim. The familiar KJV renders it LORD God. This project renders YHWH as the Eternal and Elohim as the Divine.

verses.restored: before any shrub of the field was yet on the earth, and before any plant of the field had yet sprung up, for the Eternal Divine had not sent rain upon the earth, and there was no human to serve the ground.

familiar.text: And every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and every herb of the field before it grew: for the LORD God had not caused it to rain upon the earth, and there was not a man to till the ground.

notes.title: No human to serve the ground
notes.body: The verb is often translated till or work. Serve keeps the relationship between human and ground from becoming merely extractive.

verses.restored: But a mist rose from the earth and watered the whole face of the ground.

familiar.text: But there went up a mist from the earth, and watered the whole face of the ground.

notes.title: Mist or stream
notes.body: The Hebrew word is rare. It may refer to a mist, stream, or underground flow. Mist is retained as the familiar traditional rendering, with uncertainty noted.

verses.restored: Then the Eternal Divine formed the human from dust of the ground, and breathed into the human’s nostrils the breath of life; and the human became a living being.

familiar.text: And the LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and man became a living soul.

notes.title: Human from ground
notes.body: Adam and adamah are word-linked: the human is the earth-being formed from the ground.

notes.title: Living being, not disembodied soul
notes.body: Nephesh chayyah means living being or living creature. It does not require the later idea of an immortal soul separate from the body.

notes.title: Breath of life
notes.body: Life begins as Divine breath entering earth matter. The body is not opposed to spirit; it is animated by breath.

verses.restored: And the Eternal Divine planted a garden in Eden, in the east, and placed there the human who had been formed.

familiar.text: And the LORD God planted a garden eastward in Eden; and there he put the man whom he had formed.

notes.title: Garden in Eden
notes.body: Eden is not merely the garden; the garden is planted in Eden. Eden carries the sense of delight, abundance, or pleasure.

verses.restored: And out of the ground the Eternal Divine caused every tree to spring up, pleasant to sight and good for food, and the tree of life in the middle of the garden, and the tree of the knowledge of good and harm.

familiar.text: And out of the ground made the LORD God to grow every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food; the tree of life also in the midst of the garden, and the tree of knowledge of good and evil.

notes.title: Good and harm
notes.body: The familiar phrase is good and evil. Harm is used here to keep ra from becoming a purely abstract moral category too quickly; it can mean evil, bad, harm, trouble, or adversity by context.

notes.title: Desire and nourishment
notes.body: The trees are pleasant to sight and good for food before prohibition enters the story.

verses.restored: A river went out from Eden to water the garden, and from there it divided and became four headwaters.

familiar.text: And a river went out of Eden to water the garden; and from thence it was parted, and became into four heads.

notes.title: Four headwaters
notes.body: The garden is imagined as a source from which life-giving waters divide outward.

verses.restored: The name of the first is Pishon; it is the one that winds through the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold.

familiar.text: The name of the first is Pison: that is it which compasseth the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold;

verses.restored: And the gold of that land is good. Bdellium and onyx stone are there.

familiar.text: And the gold of that land is good: there is bdellium and the onyx stone.

notes.title: Precious materials
notes.body: The garden account includes material beauty: gold, resin or aromatic substance, and stone. Matter is not treated as spiritually worthless.

verses.restored: The name of the second river is Gihon; it is the one that winds through the whole land of Cush.

familiar.text: And the name of the second river is Gihon: the same is it that compasseth the whole land of Ethiopia.

notes.title: Cush
notes.body: KJV uses Ethiopia. Cush can refer to regions associated with Nubia/Ethiopia or other lands depending on context. Cush is preserved to avoid over-narrowing.

verses.restored: The name of the third river is Tigris; it goes east of Asshur. And the fourth river is Euphrates.

familiar.text: And the name of the third river is Hiddekel: that is it which goeth toward the east of Assyria. And the fourth river is Euphrates.

notes.title: Tigris
notes.body: Hiddekel is traditionally identified with the Tigris. The familiar ancient name can remain in a note while the main text uses the recognizable river name.

verses.restored: And the Eternal Divine took the human and settled the human in the garden of Eden to serve it and keep it.

familiar.text: And the LORD God took the man, and put him into the garden of Eden to dress it and to keep it.

notes.title: Serve and keep
notes.body: The verbs avad and shamar mean serve/work and keep/guard. Human vocation begins as service and guardianship of the garden.

verses.restored: And the Eternal Divine commanded the human, saying, From every tree of the garden you may freely eat.

familiar.text: And the LORD God commanded the man, saying, Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat:

notes.title: Permission before prohibition
notes.body: The command begins with abundance: from every tree you may freely eat. The restriction comes after a wide permission.

verses.restored: But from the tree of the knowledge of good and harm, you shall not eat from it; for in the day you eat from it, dying you shall die.

familiar.text: But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die.

notes.title: Dying you shall die
notes.body: The Hebrew intensifies the verb: dying you shall die. It can express certainty of mortality or the entrance into death’s process, not necessarily instant physical collapse that same day.

notes.title: Good and harm
notes.body: The phrase is traditionally good and evil. Harm keeps open the range of ra as badness, harm, trouble, or adversity.

verses.restored: And the Eternal Divine said, It is not good for the human to be alone. I will make a helper corresponding to the human.

familiar.text: And the LORD God said, It is not good that the man should be alone; I will make him an help meet for him.

notes.title: Not good
notes.body: This is the first thing called not good: isolation.

notes.title: Helper corresponding
notes.body: Ezer does not mean servant or assistant-wife. It is also used elsewhere for powerful aid, including divine help. Kenegdo means corresponding to, opposite, facing, or matching.

verses.restored: And from the ground the Eternal Divine formed every living being of the field and every winged creature of the heavens, and brought them to the human to see what the human would call them. Whatever the human called each living being, that was its name.

familiar.text: And out of the ground the LORD God formed every beast of the field, and every fowl of the air; and brought them unto Adam to see what he would call them: and whatsoever Adam called every living creature, that was the name thereof.

notes.title: Naming
notes.body: Naming here reflects recognition and relationship, not necessarily domination.

notes.title: From the ground
notes.body: The animals are also formed from the ground, linking human and creature life to the same earth-source.

verses.restored: And the human gave names to all the herd animals, to the winged creatures of the heavens, and to every living being of the field; but for the human no helper corresponding to the human was found.

familiar.text: And Adam gave names to all cattle, and to the fowl of the air, and to every beast of the field; but for Adam there was not found an help meet for him.

notes.title: No corresponding partner
notes.body: The animals are named, but none is found as the human’s corresponding partner. The issue is relational fit, not hierarchy.

verses.restored: So the Eternal Divine caused a deep sleep to fall upon the human, and the human slept; and the Divine took one of the human’s sides and closed up the flesh beneath it.

familiar.text: And the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon Adam, and he slept: and he took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh instead thereof;

notes.title: Side, not necessarily rib
notes.body: Tsela often means side, flank, or side-structure. Rib is traditional, but side may better preserve the image of one side of the human being.

notes.title: Deep sleep
notes.body: Tardemah is a deep, divinely induced sleep, often connected with visionary or sacred transition.

verses.restored: And the Eternal Divine built the side taken from the human into a woman, and brought her to the human.

familiar.text: And the rib, which the LORD God had taken from man, made he a woman, and brought her unto the man.

notes.title: Built into woman
notes.body: The verb is build, not merely make. The woman is fashioned with architectural language, not treated as an afterthought.

verses.restored: And the human said, This time, bone from my bones and flesh from my flesh. This one shall be called Woman, for from Man this one was taken.

familiar.text: And Adam said, This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh: she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of Man.

notes.title: Woman and man
notes.body: Here the wordplay shifts to ishshah and ish, woman and man. This is the first point where male-specific language becomes central.

notes.title: Recognition, not ownership
notes.body: The speech recognizes shared substance: bone from bones, flesh from flesh.

verses.restored: Therefore a man leaves his father and his mother and clings to his woman, and they become one flesh.

familiar.text: Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh.

notes.title: One flesh
notes.body: The phrase expresses kinship, embodied union, and shared life. Later marriage doctrine builds on this, but the verse itself is poetic and etiological.

verses.restored: And the two of them were naked, the human and his woman, and they were not ashamed.

familiar.text: And they were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed.

notes.title: No shame
notes.body: The garden account ends this section with nakedness without shame. The body is not yet associated with guilt, fear, or concealment.
